decisive_weakness: The announcement-duration confound is uncontrolled and fatal to the core claim. The AI disclosure string ('Disclosure: this article was generated by an AI writing assistant and reviewed by a human editor') is roughly 4-5x longer in spoken duration than the human-framed string ('Written by a staff journalist'). Any condition difference in dwell-onset latency, heading-jump counts, or post-banner navigation could be fully explained by the extra seconds of speech interrupting the participant's flow, rather than by the semantic content of the disclosure. The study cannot distinguish 'AI authorship disclosure changes behavior' from 'longer audio interruptions change behavior,' which is the entire contribution.

evidence_span.quote: The human-framed condition ('Written by a staff journalist') is substantially shorter than the AI disclosure string, which introduces a confound the rehearsal cannot ignore: announcement duration differs across conditions by several seconds.

criticisms.violated_criterion: confound control: stimuli across conditions must be matched on dimensions not under test
criticisms.why_matters: Dwell-onset latency, heading-jump counts following injection, and even think-aloud acknowledgment rates will all be inflated in the AI conditions purely because the participant is forced to wait through a longer announcement. A statistically significant Friedman result on any behavioral measure will be uninterpretable: the study cannot distinguish a semantic disclosure effect from an audio-interruption-length effect. The claimed contribution — that AI provenance disclosure shifts navigation and credibility — evaporates.
criticisms.required_change: Either (a) length-match the disclosure strings to within 0.5 seconds of spoken duration (e.g., 'This article was AI-generated, human-edited' vs. 'This article was staff-written, human-edited'), verified with the actual target screen readers at participant-configured speech rates; or (b) add a fourth condition with a length-matched neutral announcement ('This article is approximately 800 words long') to isolate the interruption-length component; or (c) reframe the claim as 'disclosure presence vs. absence' and drop the human-framed condition entirely.

evidence_span.quote: Compare these measures across the three conditions (early-AI, late-AI, human) using a within-subject Friedman test across the 8 participants, with individual condition contrasts via Wilcoxon signed-rank.

criticisms.violated_criterion: statistical power adequate to the claim
criticisms.why_matters: With n=8 participants across three within-subject conditions, Friedman with post-hoc Wilcoxon has power to detect only very large effects (d > ~1.0). The one_sentence_claim asserts 'measurable' effects and that 'the effect depends on where the disclosure sits in the heading tree' — a three-way contrast requiring even more power than a two-way. A null result cannot distinguish 'no effect' from 'small-to-moderate effect the design cannot see,' yet the analysis plan presents these tests as if they can adjudicate the hypothesis. The qualitative signals cannot rescue the quantitative framing because the summed credibility score and derived latency measures are explicitly being tested for statistical significance.
criticisms.required_change: Either (a) drop the inferential statistics entirely and reframe as a descriptive/qualitative evaluation with the quantitative measures reported as trial-level observations only; or (b) perform a sensitivity power analysis stating the minimum detectable effect size at n=8 and acknowledge in the contribution that only very large effects are addressable; or (c) increase n to a pre-specified target derived from a power analysis for the smallest effect of theoretical interest.

evidence_span.quote: by the third trial, a participant would reasonably have inferred that authorship framing is the experimental variable of interest, and their credibility ratings in trial 3 could plausibly reflect hypothesis-guessing rather than authentic judgment.

criticisms.violated_criterion: demand characteristics control in within-subject designs
criticisms.why_matters: Within-subject exposure to three differently-framed disclosures on three otherwise-similar articles makes the manipulation transparent by trial 2 or 3. Latin-square counterbalancing addresses order but not hypothesis-guessing. The credibility ratings and section-selection choices in later trials will reflect what participants believe the researchers want to find, not authentic judgment. This directly undermines the credibility-rating dependent measure, which is one of the three central signals.
criticisms.required_change: Move to a between-subjects design for the credibility rating (one condition per participant, requiring a larger n), OR add an explicit cover story and post-hoc suspicion probe before debrief to identify and exclude participants who inferred the manipulation, OR restrict the credibility-rating analysis to trial 1 only and acknowledge the remaining trials as exploratory qualitative data.

evidence_span.quote: Two coders independently apply a 4-category scheme to each clip... Compute inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa) on the 24 clips (8 participants × 3 trials).

criticisms.violated_criterion: qualitative analysis rigor: coder blinding and codebook development
criticisms.why_matters: The analysis plan names Cohen's kappa but does not specify: (a) whether coders are blinded to condition assignment when coding the 20-second clips — if the clip begins with the banner audio, the coder will hear which condition it is and blinding is impossible without audio editing; (b) how disagreements are resolved; (c) whether the codebook was developed on pilot data separate from the 24 analysis clips or will be refined iteratively on the same data (which inflates kappa). Without these specifications the qualitative inter-rater statistic is not interpretable.
criticisms.required_change: Specify: (i) the banner audio will be excised from clips before coding to enable blinding, or state explicitly that blinding is impossible and justify; (ii) the disagreement-resolution protocol (e.g., third-coder adjudication); (iii) that the codebook will be finalized on pilot/warm-up data before the 24 analysis clips are coded, and report kappa on the locked codebook only.

evidence_span.quote: Determine from the screen-share and NVDA speech viewer overlay when the participant's virtual cursor has passed the second H2 heading, as the late-banner trigger timing depends on this cursor position rather than elapsed time.

criticisms.violated_criterion: manipulation fidelity: the experimental condition must be delivered as specified
criticisms.why_matters: The walkthrough notes that a fast navigator can traverse all three H2s in under 4 seconds, giving the wizard essentially no window to fire the late-banner between H2 #2 and H2 #3. When the wizard misses the window, the 'late' condition collapses into a variable-timing condition — sometimes after H2 #2, sometimes after H2 #3, sometimes during body reading. The branch's core claim that 'the effect depends on where the disclosure sits in the heading tree' is precisely a claim about timing, and timing is exactly what the wizard cannot reliably deliver. Manipulation fidelity is unverified.
criticisms.required_change: Either (a) automate the late-banner trigger using a DOM-based heading-focus event listener (JavaScript focus/visibility tracking on the H2 element) rather than wizard-eyeballing the screen-share, with the wizard retaining only a veto override; or (b) log a per-trial manipulation-fidelity measure (seconds from second-H2-pass to banner-fire) and pre-register an exclusion threshold; or (c) drop the early/late distinction and test disclosure-presence vs. absence only.

evidence_span.quote: does disclosing AI authorship via an ARIA-live provenance banner shift BLV screen-reader users' navigation strategies and credibility judgments

criticisms.violated_criterion: sampling adequacy to the population the claim addresses
criticisms.why_matters: The claim is about 'BLV screen-reader users' as a population, but n=8 recruited from a single disability-led community organization cannot support a population-level claim. BLV screen-reader users vary enormously in assistive-tech configuration (NVDA vs. VoiceOver vs. JAWS), onset (congenital vs. acquired blindness), age, and browsing expertise. The sample is a convenience sample whose configurations are deliberately heterogeneous (each participant uses their own setup), which introduces variance that the n=8 cannot absorb. No stopping rule, saturation criterion, or sampling framework is stated.
criticisms.required_change: Reframe the contribution explicitly as an exploratory case series (n=8 expert users from one community organization), drop population-level language, and state a qualitative saturation criterion (e.g., thematic saturation across the debrief corpus) rather than implying the findings generalize to BLV screen-reader users broadly. Alternatively, expand recruitment across multiple organizations and screen-reader platforms with a pre-specified stratification.
